fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

CAMPTON J:

By way of an Initiating Application filed on 9 December 2025 in the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 2), Ms Hasso (“the applicant”) seeks a decree of nullity of her marriage to Mr Izak (“the respondent”) entered on [...] 2024 pursuant to s 23B of the Marriage Act 1961 (Cth) (“the Marriage Act”) and s 44(1A) of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”). The application is based on the contention that her consent to the marriage was vitiated by duress and hence was not real consent.
The respondent by way of his Response to an Initiating Application filed on 10 February 2026 seeks for the application to be dismissed and costs. The proceeding was transferred to Division 1 on 23 February 2026.

BACKGROUND

The applicant was born in 1984 and is currently 41 years old. The respondent was born in 1981 and is currently 44 years old. The applicant is a citizen of China. The respondent is a citizen of Australia. The respondent was previously married in 2017 and has one child from another relationship. There are no children of the relationship of the parties.
The applicant arrived in Australia on a visitor visa in July 2023. A condition of her visa prohibited her from engaging in employment. She has very limited English skills. She had the assistance of a Mandarin interpreter on the day of the hearing.
The respondent met the applicant around September 2023. They commenced a relationship on 24 December 2023.
The marriage certificate (Exhibit 1) establishes that on [...] 2024 the applicant and the respondent entered a marriage, solemnised by an authorised marriage celebrant, pursuant to the Marriage Act. The marriage certificate records the conjugal status of both the applicant and the respondent as “Divorced”.

ORAL APPLICATION – ADJOURNMENT

At the commencement of the hearing, the applicant made an oral application for an adjournment for the following reasons:
(a) She did not understand the hearing process and had insufficient time to obtain legal representation for the purposes of the hearing;

(b) She had very limited English language skills; and

(c) In identifying s 102NA of the Act, she said that the integrity of any oral evidence would be adversely impacted because of family violence.

The respondent opposed the adjournment.
The oral application for an adjournment was refused for the following reasons:
(a) The applicant herself, more than three months ago, prepared the affidavit material relied upon in the prosecution of the nullity application. That material utilises not unsophisticated language, exhibiting an understanding as to the relevant legal and factual matters applicable to the relief sought.

(b) The proceeding was listed before a judicial registrar on 11 February 2026 and 23 February 2026. No caveat to the allocation of hearing dates is recorded in the orders.

(c) The applicant attended on Legal Aid and was advised that they could not assist. She had not made any application for a grant of Legal Aid.

(d) The material relied upon by each of the applicant and the respondent does not trigger any mandatory application of s 102NA of the Act. On enquiry, the applicant could not identify any material in her affidavit to support a discretionary consideration to apply the provisions of s 102NA. The procedure of the hearing was outlined to the applicant in plain language.

(e) Mr B with whom the applicant has lived with since 8 September 2025 and who was a witness in her case was available at the hearing to assist her at the bar table in the conduct of the proceeding.

(f) The Court directed and had met the cost of a mandarin interpreter to be present at the hearing for the applicant.

(g) The respondent had met the cost of briefing counsel. The applicant’s material did not disclose that she could meet any costs thrown away by the respondent in the event an adjournment was granted on condition.

Material relied upon by each party

The applicant relied on:
(a) Her Initiating Application filed on 9 December 2025;

(b) Her affidavit filed on 9 December 2025; and

(c) The affidavit of Mr B filed on 9 December 2025.

The respondent relied on:
(a) His Response to an Initiating Application filed on 10 February 2026; and

(b) His affidavit filed on 10 February 2026.

Neither party wished to cross-examine the deponent of any affidavits. The application was determined on the basis of the untested evidence and submissions.

THE LAW

Section 51 of the Act provides that an application for a decree of nullity shall be on the ground that the marriage in question is void. Pursuant to s 23B(1) of the Marriage Act, a marriage is void where, inter alia:
23B Grounds on which marriages are void

...

(d) the consent of either of the parties is not a real consent because:
(i) it was obtained by duress or fraud;
In In the marriage of Teves III and Campomayor [1994] FamCA 57; (1995) FLC 92-578, Lindenmayer J stated (at 81,737) that:
...duress does not necessarily need to involve a direct threat of physical violence so long as there is sufficient oppression, from whatever source, acting upon a party to vitiate the reality of their consent.
That said, Lindenmayer J also highlighted the need for there to be some proximity between the alleged duress and the marriage ceremony itself, (at 81,739):
... it is duress at the time of the marriage ceremony that is critical. Clearly this can be induced by events prior to it, but in the end it is for the applicant to show that at the time she gave her consent at the ceremony, some overbearing force was operating.
The applicant bears the onus of proof to establish, based on admissible evidence, the facts and circumstances said to ground the declaration (see Axon v Axon (1937) 50 CLR 395 at [40]). The grant of a decree of nullity is not discretionary. If the facts establish the marriage is invalid, relief must issue.
The standard of proof required is on the balance of probabilities as identified in s 140(2) of the Evidence Act 1995 (Cth) having regard to the gravity of the matters alleged. Assertions, conclusions, indefinite testimony and indirect inferences are insufficient to ground a finding of fact.

Consideration

Upon travelling to Australia, the applicant commenced employment in personal services. She did not give context as to how this employment evolved, saying that she was soon working “24 hours a day, 7 days a week” and consuming methamphetamine (ice) to remain awake and to meet her work schedule. She concedes that she became “fully addicted” to consuming ice daily. The applicant said that her employer, “the boss”, withheld her wages, isolated her from other workers and threatened her with deportation to China.
The applicant purported to give hearsay evidence as to “the boss” explaining a “payment structure” by way of a fee to obtain the benefit of immigration status by entering into a marriage to remain living in this country. She said that on 9 May 2024, the fee that she was to pay to “the boss” was $45,000 plus a $3,500 intermediary fee, and that “the boss” instructed her to fabricate evidence of a relationship with the respondent. The applicant asserts that a direct nexus exists between what she described as her escalating exploitation by “the boss” and the entry into the marriage. I give this hearsay evidence no weight. While the applicant adduced evidence as to monies being transferred to and from a joint account of she and the respondent payable to “[Ms C]”, asserting that “[Ms C]” is “the boss”, the first name and surname of “the boss” was not disclosed. The source of the hearsay evidence is opaque. The applicant, who on her evidence left the arrangement with “the boss” during August 2025 to commence living in the home of Mr B, had the capacity to issue a subpoena directed to “the boss” to give evidence at the hearing. She failed to do so. I infer that evidence would not assist her case.
The respondent strenuously denies the serious allegation as to any arrangement or complicity between he and any person to marry the applicant for the purposes of financial gain or immigration advantage.
The applicant did not adduce evidence as to the circumstances of her first meeting with the respondent or any detail as to their interactions between May 2024 until the marriage in late 2024. In contrast, the respondent said he met the applicant at a friend’s dinner at a restaurant in the city in September 2023. He was aware at that time as to her engagement in personal services. He gives evidence as to meeting and spending time with the applicant after that initial exchange once or twice a week, including having lunch and going shopping, and introducing her to his son. He adduced photographs of those meetings into evidence.
In May 2024, the parties opened a joint bank account. They each deposited money into the account and used it to pay for day-to-day expenses including food and groceries. As part of their marriage plans, the respondent agreed to sponsor the applicant’s partner visa for immigration purposes. He says the person “[Ms C]”, identified in the joint account bank statements, is an immigration agent. In November 2024, the applicant and the respondent instructed Ms D to prepare and lodge an application for a partnership visa application.
In November 2024, the respondent paid $3,800 from the joint account to meet the costs of the partnership visa for the applicant. The applicant did not directly put the respondent’s evidence as to the detail of their interactions from May 2024 until the marriage in issue. There is no probative reason not to accept the respondent’s evidence.
The applicant’s assertions are not admissible evidence. She has not established to the requisite degree the serious contention that the respondent was “not an innocent party to the marriage” and that he “actively participated in “‘the boss’ operation”.
As to the applicant establishing to the requisite degree the proximity of circumstances which, taken together, lead to the conclusion that because of a form of coercion or oppression, she has not exercised a voluntary consent to the marriage on [...] 2024, and hence that consent is vitiated by duress and is not a real consent, the extent of her evidence is:
On [...] 2024, under cumulative duress-the threat of deportation, having already paid the boss approximately $123,000 AUD, the psychological manipulation that made me believe she was my only protector, and the demands of the 24/7 work schedule-I signed marriage documents at [F Street], [Suburb G] NSW, before marriage celebrant [Ms E]...
My consent was not freely given. The lack of sleep, ongoing drug use, being confined to the premises 24 hours a day, and the psychological conditioning that made me believe I had no choice, all impaired my ability to think clearly and choose freely. The marriage was an artificial transaction orchestrated by the boss. The Respondent and I never lived together or had a genuine relationship.
The respondent gives specific evidence as to the circumstances of the marriage ceremony on [...] 2024, including the applicant’s friends attending, the ceremony lasting an hour and the disposition as to the applicant being smiling, hugging, and happy. He also adduced into evidence photographs of the ceremony.
The applicant had the capacity to adduce evidence as to the particulars and context of the events leading up to the marriage ceremony, including her interactions with “the boss”. For reasons that are not explained, she failed to do so. The applicant does not set out in detail the circumstances of the ceremony on the day of the wedding. She does not put into issue the respondent’s evidence as to the circumstances and events on that day. There is no reason not to accept the respondent’s evidence.
Cogent evidence must be given before a presumption of consent can be rebutted and a marriage annulled if, as here, an adult has gone through a public ceremony giving all the indications of consent (Cooper (falsely called Crane) v Crane [1891] P 369 at 375-377 (“Cooper”); Azmi & Shinde [2014] FamCA 824). In Cooper, the Court said:
...In order to hold that the ceremony so performed was not binding, I think I should have to infer as a fact one of two things - either that she was so perturbed by terror that her mind was unhinged and she did not understand what she was doing ...or that though she understood what she was doing her powers of volition were so paralysed that, by her words and acts, she merely gave expression to the will of the respondent and not her own.
The applicant travelled to Country H with the respondent for two days in January 2025. She then immediately resumed her 24/7 work schedule. She says, absent context, that on 25 July 2025, the “marriage visa price” had increased from $150,000 to $210,000.
The applicant met Mr B on 3 May 2025. She has been living full-time in his home since 8 September 2025. I give his evidence as to events that occurred prior to May 2025 little weight in circumstances where he deposes that the information contained in his affidavit as to these historical matters are grounded from his conclusions drawn from documents and other unspecified sources.
The respondent asserts that he and the applicant were in a genuine marriage relationship until the applicant told him she was pursuing a new relationship. As to the circumstances of the breakdown of the relationship, the respondent gives the following evidence:
From around July or August 2025, [the applicant] talked about a customer she met at work, who came to see her regularly. I was not worried when [the applicant] would tell me such stories, as I thought it was just a customer.
However, in early September 2025, I went to her workplace to take her to lunch again. After we met, we had a conversation in words to the following effect:
Me: ready to go?

Applicant: No. I am not going. I am leaving you. You do not have a house or any money.

Me: what are you talking about?

Applicant: I have found someone else. He is a local white guy and he has a property.

Me: is that the customer you kept talking about?

Applicant: yes. You should go, I do not want him to see me with you.
I was in shock. Like all other couples, we had occasional minor arguments, but I did not expect this from her. I left the premises.
After I left, the communication between [the applicant] and I broke down. I was extremely upset and felt that [the applicant] had just used me for a VISA as opposed to having any real feelings for me. It became apparent to me that she was happy to find somebody more affluent to sponsor her, without any regard for our relationship.
On [...] 2025, I signed a withdrawal of sponsorship form and submitted it to the Department of Home Affairs.
I did not have any contact with [the applicant] until I received court documents filed in December 2025.
(Emphasis in original)
The applicant said in her affidavit:
I voluntarily reported my visa, work, and marriage circumstances to the Department of Home Affairs. This disclosure was made voluntarily and supports my credibility in these proceedings.
I find on a consideration of the evidence that the applicant has not established to the requisite degree that she was in some way compelled by the general circumstances or the alleged behaviour and conduct of “the boss” or the respondent to enter the marriage. I am not satisfied that the applicant has established the ground for relief. The application will be dismissed.
Neither party was given the opportunity to make submissions as to costs. Directions will be made to facilitate such applications should they be pressed. The seriousness of the contentions in this matter made by the applicant directs that consideration be given to referral of the court file to the Department of Home Affairs, notwithstanding the applicant’s voluntary disclosure as identified in paragraph 53 of her affidavit.
For all of the above reasons, I make the following orders.
